title: Hollywood Thrives on 'Rabid' Fans. For Publicists, They're a Nightmare
gist: As celebrity fandoms grow more intense and digitally empowered, publicists increasingly function as crisis managers and quasi-security teams, navigating a paradox where deeply engaged fans are both essential marketing assets and potential liability sources. The article explores how social media has transformed fan access and the 24/7 nature of PR, using examples from Chappell Roan, Timothée Chalamet stans, and the Heated Rivalry fandom to illustrate the risks of mismanaging parasocial relationships.

insights.claim: Large fan accounts regularly eclipse traditional press in audience reach, making them de facto marketing partners that publicists actively cultivate with exclusive content and curated event invitations.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Fan-driven organic marketing is displacing paid traditional media spend for celebrity brands, reshaping entertainment PR budgets.
insights.quote: In my experience fan pages will eclipse traditional press in huge numbers. Fans are essentially doing a large-scale marketing operation for you a lot of the time.
insights.locator: Paragraph on fan accounts as marketing

insights.claim: Publicists now reward high-traffic fan accounts with exclusive content previews and event invitations as a deliberate strategy to harness grassroots marketing at scale.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Entertainment companies that systematically cultivate fan influencer networks can reduce conventional marketing costs while gaining highly credible promotional reach.
insights.quote: publicists will often reach out to large fan accounts with exclusive offers to post a snippet from an artist's upcoming music video or invite them to a curated event
insights.locator: Fan accounts as marketing partners paragraph

insights.claim: Intensely engaged fandoms can turn on a celebrity rapidly, as illustrated when a healthy food influencer's fans swarmed comments against an applesauce brand partnership they deemed off-brand, forcing the team to repost with the product less prominently featured.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Brand partnerships for influencers and celebrities carry meaningful fandom-backlash risk that can undermine the commercial deal, requiring scenario planning before signing.
insights.quote: Her fans swarmed the comments section, outraged that she would collaborate with a product they perceived to be unhealthy and a deviation from her brand.

insights.claim: Celebrities must balance providing fans enough access to feel rewarded while avoiding so much access that it triggers safety incidents or reputational crises, with publicists acting as the primary calibrators of this threshold.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Access management is becoming a core competency for celebrity brand management, with measurable downside risk from both under- and over-engagement with fans.
insights.quote: There's always this balance you're dealing with, because you don't want to alienate your fans, and you can't get the reputation of somebody being mean or shitty to them. But at the same time, the fans are rabid.

themes.theme: Social Media Transforming Celebrity PR Operations

insights.claim: Social media has converted celebrity public relations from a scheduled, campaign-based function into a continuous 24/7 monitoring operation covering TikTok, subreddits, and Instagram comments.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: PR agencies serving celebrities face structurally higher operating costs due to always-on digital monitoring requirements, compressing margins unless headcount or tooling scales accordingly.
insights.quote: You're constantly looking at TikToks, subreddit comments, Instagram comments.

insights.claim: Fan geo-tracking via celebrity social media posts has become a genuine physical safety threat, forcing publicists to monitor and sometimes demand deletion of posts that inadvertently reveal real-time locations.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Safety liability exposure for talent management firms is rising as fan tracking capabilities improve, creating demand for location-aware social media protocols.
insights.quote: We've almost started acting like security, which can be a little bit scary. She says she once had to call an artist and beg her to take down a post because it accidentally revealed what NYC street corner she was standing on.

insights.claim: During the Sway House era, influencers Josh Richards and Bryce Hall could post a TikTok in a public location and have hundreds of fans physically show up at that location within minutes.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Real-time fan mobilization via short-form video creates unpredictable crowd-control scenarios that event venues and security operations must now anticipate for creator-affiliated events.
insights.quote: There'd just be hundreds of kids showing up being like, 'Oh my God, so-and-so's on the corner of Sunset.'

insights.claim: Publicists now function as quasi-security personnel by screening all client social media posts for inadvertent location disclosures before publication.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Talent management scope creep into security functions signals potential market opportunity for dedicated social media safety tech products aimed at celebrity teams.
insights.quote: We have to be involved with absolutely everything they post.

themes.theme: Fandom Crisis Management and Reputational Risk

insights.claim: Chappell Roan's public statement saying fans made her feel 'loved but unsafe' generated significant fan backlash in 2024, illustrating that even carefully worded acknowledgments of fan intensity can create a narrative of ingratitude.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Celebrity communication strategies around fan boundaries carry reputational risk even when justified, suggesting a market need for crisis scenario planning specific to parasocial dynamics.
insights.quote: Roan famously got flak when she released a statement in 2024 saying her fans made her feel 'loved' but 'unsafe.'

insights.claim: A misattributed security incident involving Chappell Roan and a Brazilian footballer's daughter in early 2026 demonstrates how parasocial fandom amplifies and entrenches negative narratives even when the celebrity is later exonerated.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Reputational damage from fandom-adjacent incidents persists even after factual correction, indicating that pre-emptive relationship management is more effective than reactive crisis communication.
insights.quote: the narrative that she was ungrateful to her fans had already been seeded.

insights.claim: PR professionals assess a three-day negative fan news cycle as equivalent to 'three years' worth of reputational damage in practical management terms.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: The asymmetric reputational cost of fan-related crises justifies disproportionate upfront investment in fan relationship management relative to the short duration of the actual news cycle.
insights.quote: That was a three-day news story, but in PR Land, that's like three fucking years.

insights.claim: Stars François Arnaud and Hudson Williams of Heated Rivalry publicly denounced toxic fan behavior via Instagram stories, demonstrating that proactive fandom statements can be strategically appropriate when behavior crosses into racism, homophobia, or harassment.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Celebrities who publicly draw ethical lines around fan conduct may differentiate their brands positively in an era of growing public scrutiny of parasocial dynamics.
insights.quote: Don't call yourself a fan if you share racist/ homophobic/ biphobic/ misogynistic/ ageist/ ableist/ parasocial/ bigoted comments of any kind.

insights.claim: Connor Storrie's publicists advised against any public comment on the violent fan altercation involving Club Chalamet, on the basis that engagement would associate him with bad behavior rather than distance him from it.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: The default PR playbook for celebrity-adjacent fan conflicts is silence, which preserves brand separation but may leave narratives to develop without control.
insights.quote: There's no benefit to him commenting. It just associates you with people who are behaving badly.

themes.theme: Parasocial Economy and the Shift in Narrative Control

insights.claim: The balance of narrative control over celebrities has shifted toward fandoms, with management teams forced to reactively monitor and respond to fan-generated sentiment rather than driving the agenda.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Talent agencies and PR firms face structurally weakening control over client narratives, which could pressure their value proposition and pricing power versus social-native fan management services.

insights.claim: Publicists advise celebrities to make public statements about fan harassment only when it reaches specific thresholds — credible threats of violence or clearly discriminatory language — not for routine fan conflict.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: Threshold-based response frameworks suggest PR firms are developing formal decision trees for fandom crisis response, an emerging professional discipline with codifiable best practices.
insights.quote: I do think we as publicists have the responsibility to advise our clients when they should make a statement. You can't control what your fans say, but you can always control what you say.

insights.claim: Internet-empowered fan bases represent a structural double-edged sword for celebrities: they are the primary income source while simultaneously creating the greatest reputational and safety risk.
insights.speaker: Ej Dickson
insights.so_what: The monetization model built on intense fan loyalty contains inherent systemic risk that is not yet fully priced into talent valuation or management contracts.
insights.quote: For celebrities, a large, devoted fan base is a double-edged sword: It's a source of their income, but it also can be a huge pain in the ass for them and their teams.
